bull_thesis: Applied Optoelectronics looks like a genuine turning point in the AI build-out. Datacenter revenue grew about 154% year-over-year in the first quarter of fiscal 2026, making up 54% of a record $151.1M in total revenue. Management raised full-year 2026 guidance to more than $1.1B in revenue with over $140M in non-GAAP operating income (operating profit before certain one-time and stock-based items), and pointed to a $124M order book for 800G and 1.6T transceivers from a single large cloud-computing customer since mid-March 2026. The setup is the strongest in the company's history. First, it is now qualified at the two biggest AI build-outs: a multi-year Microsoft supply agreement plus a deal and warrant with Amazon (up to $4B of potential purchases, and a warrant for 7.95M shares at $23.70), which lifts the company from a small cable-optics maker into a US-based supplier of AI connectivity. Second, the chief executive puts real demand at $1.4-1.5B, genuinely beyond what the company can build through mid-2027, so the limit is its own capacity rather than customer appetite, which is good for pricing and how fully its plants run. Third, it makes its own indium-phosphide lasers in-house (a 350% expansion in wafer capacity, moving from 4-inch to 6-inch wafers by the end of 2027), a scarce asset when that chip capacity is the whole industry's binding constraint. Fourth, its tilt toward US manufacturing (in Texas, more than 50% US by the end of 2027) is a hedge against tariffs and a fit for government-backed AI that competitors lack. Fifth, the balance sheet is far safer after the 2025 fundraises and a swap of its convertible debt, leaving roughly $195M in net cash (cash minus debt). Sixth, its work on co-packaged optics and a new pluggable design (400,000 units a month by the end of 2027, with three-year supply agreements under negotiation) gives it a seat in the next generation of the technology. Gross margin (the share of revenue left after the direct cost of goods) has climbed from 17.8% to 30.0% between fiscal 2021 and fiscal 2025, with a guided path to 35% by the end of 2026 and over 40% in 2027. If the second-half ramp of 60-80% sequential growth lands and margins turn up, the mid-2027 datacenter run-rate of about $471M a month would imply a multi-billion-dollar revenue base at widening margins. The valuation is rich, but it is in line with Coherent (price-to-earnings of 186 times) and Lumentum (159 times) in a group where the market is willing to pay almost any price for AI-connectivity exposure.
bear_thesis: Applied Optoelectronics has never earned a GAAP operating profit (an operating profit under standard accounting rules) in its history as a public company. Every year from fiscal 2021 through the trailing twelve months has been a loss: a fiscal 2025 operating margin of -12.0%, -6.7% over the trailing twelve months, and a non-GAAP loss of $0.07 per share in the first quarter of fiscal 2026. Free cash flow (the cash left after running and investing in the business) over the trailing twelve months is -$418M, or -$6.27 a share, so the entire ramp is being paid for by burning roughly $400M of cash a year and diluting shareholders by more than 50% year-over-year, with the Amazon warrant set to add up to 7.95M more shares. At about $162 a share and a market value of roughly $13B on about $507M of trailing revenue, the stock trades at about 26 times EV/sales (enterprise value, the equity value plus net debt, divided by revenue) for a money-losing maker of commodity hardware with gross margins under 30% — roughly five times the EV/sales of Fabrinet (5.1 times), its closest contract-optics comparison. The whole valuation treats a mid-2027 run-rate as if it were already certain. The product is built to fall in price over time: every step up in speed (100G to 400G to 800G to 1.6T) has historically squeezed selling prices and pushed cloud customers to buy from more than one supplier, and the company's discrete-laser approach is exposed to being replaced by co-packaged optics, silicon photonics, or linear-drive optics routed through silicon from Marvell, Coherent, or Nvidia-aligned designs. Customer concentration is severe: datacenter is more than 70% of revenue and sits with about two cloud customers, and the company's own history shows that losing a single customer's design slot can gut the stock. The $140M operating-income and 35% gross-margin targets are non-GAAP, loaded into the second half — exactly the kind of forecast the company has historically missed. The stock fell 19% (regular plus after-hours trading) on the May 7 report despite a guidance raise, which tells you expectations are already stretched. The 800G award and the Amazon and Microsoft relationships are already public and largely in the price; this is a catalyst that has already happened and is playing out, not a fresh, lopsided one.
sop_bull: The bull sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) is about $185-210 a share. It uses forward EV/sales (enterprise value divided by revenue) on the more-than-$1.1B fiscal 2026 guide for the whole company. Datacenter (about 70% of fiscal 2026 revenue, roughly $770M, AI connectivity growing more than 150%) is valued at about 12 times forward EV/sales — a premium toward the Credo and Coherent group for cloud-scale exposure — for about $9.2B of enterprise value. The cable-TV business (about $325M in fiscal 2026, stable, growing about 4%) is valued at about 2.5 times for roughly $0.8B. Telecom (about $10M, winding down) is about $0.1B. That is roughly $10.1B of operating enterprise value. Adding net cash (cash minus debt) of about $195M gives equity of about $10.3B; across roughly 80M shares that is about $129 a share on the guide. The bull then layers on the mid-2027 datacenter run-rate of about $471M a month ($5.6B a year) at 35-40% gross margin, applies a forward run-rate sales multiple of about 3-4 times, and gets to about $185-210 a share on a 2027 exit. The Amazon warrant (which triggers up to $4B of purchases) and the co-packaged-optics options are upside not included in the base case.
sop_bear: The bear sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) is about $45-70 a share. One approach applies a contract-optics, Fabrinet-style EV/sales (enterprise value divided by revenue) of about 5 times to a cut-down fiscal 2026 revenue of about $1.0B (if the second-half ramp slips, the business is still losing money under standard accounting, and gross margin stays under 30%), for about $5.0B of enterprise value. Another values it on a normalized basis at about 7-8 times a believable 2027 operating profit of roughly $120-150M (if margins turn up to about 30% on each new dollar of revenue), discounted for execution and dilution risk. By segment: datacenter at about 6 times sales (a commodity-transceiver discount for falling selling prices and customer concentration) on about $770M is roughly $4.6B; cable-TV at about 1.5 times on $325M is roughly $0.5B; plus net cash of $195M, less the full dilution of the 7.95M Amazon warrant shares and continued share issuance. That puts bear equity at about $3.6-5.5B, and across roughly 85-90M fully diluted shares, about $45-70 a share. A failed second-half ramp or the loss of a single cloud customer would re-rate the stock toward the Fabrinet multiple quickly.
sop_breakdown: The base case blends the growing datacenter franchise and the safer balance sheet against the still-unproven profitability and the long tail of falling selling prices. Datacenter (about $770M of fiscal 2026 revenue, AI connectivity, qualified at the big cloud customers) is valued at about 9 times forward EV/sales (enterprise value divided by revenue), for about $6.9B of enterprise value — a premium to Fabrinet (5.1 times) for the quality of its design wins, and a discount to Credo and Coherent (silicon-chip names at about 40 times price-to-earnings) for the company's commodity-laser mix and its losses under standard accounting. The cable-TV business (about $325M, a steady cash generator) is valued at about 2.5 times for roughly $0.8B. Telecom is about $0.1B. That is about $7.8B of operating enterprise value; adding net cash (cash minus debt) of about $195M gives roughly $8.0B of equity, and across about 80M shares, about $100 a share. The Amazon warrant and the co-packaged-optics potential are held as upside rather than counted in the base; the steady dilution is the offsetting downside. The center is about $105, with a range of $95-115 — meaningfully below the roughly $162 market price, meaning the AI build-out upside is already more than fully reflected in the stock.
risk_reward: Unfavorable at about $162. Measured against fair value, the roughly $105 base case implies about -35% (the stock sits above what we think it is worth). The bull case of about $185-210 is +14% to +30%, but it requires the full second-half ramp of 60-80%, a margin turn, and no lost design slot all together. The downside to the bear case of about $45-70 is -57% to -72%. The risk and reward are tilted to the downside — roughly two to three times more room to fall to the bear case than to rise to the bull case — because the upside has effectively been paid for in advance. It only becomes attractive after a deep sell-off.
peer_comps_note: The true AI build-out optical peers (checked live in June 2026): Coherent at about 186 times trailing price-to-earnings with about 40% non-GAAP gross margin, the diversified leader; Lumentum at about 159 times price-to-earnings, the number-two in lasers and datacom; Fabrinet at a forward price-to-earnings of about 44, EV/sales (enterprise value divided by revenue) of about 5.1 times, and enterprise value of about $24.9B, the contract-manufacturing comparison closest to Applied Optoelectronics' hardware economics; and Credo at a forward price-to-earnings of about 41 and enterprise value of about $40.3B, the adjacent name in active electrical cables and high-speed chips. JPMorgan frames Coherent and Lumentum at about 25 times 2028 earnings on about 40% growth. Within this group, Applied Optoelectronics is the smallest, most volatile, and the only one that loses money year after year (its operating margin under standard accounting is negative every year, and its gross margin of about 29% is the lowest in the group versus about 40%-plus at Coherent and Credo). On valuation it is the most stretched on EV/sales (about 26 times on $507M of trailing revenue versus Fabrinet's 5.1 times), precisely because it has no earnings to anchor a price-to-earnings ratio. On growth it ranks highest (datacenter up about 154% year-over-year, total up 51-64%) off the smallest base. On returns it ranks last (negative return on invested capital and free cash flow of -$418M over the trailing twelve months). On exposure to AI capital spending it is a high-sensitivity, pure-play name (datacenter is more than 70% of revenue, but concentrated in a single cloud customer). In short: the highest growth and highest volatility, but the lowest quality and the most stretched valuation per dollar of revenue.
role_in_scaleout: Applied Optoelectronics is a US-based, vertically integrated (it makes its own indium-phosphide lasers) supplier of datacenter optical transceivers, ramping 800G and 1.6T pluggable parts for large AI computing clusters. It fills a real, in-demand spot in the connectivity bottleneck of the AI build-out, but it is a role where selling prices structurally fall over time, where revenue is concentrated in a single cloud customer, and where commodity competition is a constant. Demand is durable for the group as a whole, yet fragile for this company in particular because it depends on winning specific design slots.
consensus_delta: The market is not looking the wrong way in the manner a value play needs. It is fully on board with the AI-connectivity story and is paying about 26 times EV/sales (enterprise value divided by revenue) and group multiples near 186 times for it. Where we disagree with the market here actually cuts against the stock: consensus already builds in the second-half ramp of 60-80% sequential growth, the more-than-$1.1B fiscal 2026 guide, and the mid-2027 datacenter run-rate of about $471M a month as the base case, and the chief executive's '$1.4-1.5B of real demand' figure invites the Street to model even higher. The mistaken assumptions baked into consensus are, first, that the company turns this revenue ramp into a profit under standard accounting and a 35%-plus gross margin on schedule — it has never earned an operating profit under standard accounting, and first-quarter gross margin actually slipped to 29.2% from fiscal 2025's 30.0% — and second, that having only two cloud customers and falling selling prices at each new speed are harmless. The mispricing is to the downside: the stock fell 19% on a guidance raise (May 7), which tells you expectations are saturated and good news no longer moves it up.
valley_of_death: The risky window is the next three to nine months. First, the entire fiscal 2026 story is loaded into the back half, on a 60-80% sequential ramp in the third and fourth quarters that depends on Texas capacity coming online and on cloud-customer qualification timing — any slip prints a miss into an already-stretched valuation. Second, free cash flow is -$418M over the trailing twelve months; the capital-heavy ramp keeps burning cash, and it is funded by issuing stock, so expect continued dilution plus the overhang of the 7.95M-share Amazon warrant. Third, losses under standard accounting persist through the build; the $140M operating-income guide is non-GAAP and unproven. Fourth, the optical group has already fallen about 15-17% over the summer of 2026 on worries about co-packaged optics being delayed and a lack of catalysts — this is the most volatile name in that group and will overshoot any sector drop. Fifth, the timing of a single customer matters: the $124M order book is one cloud customer, and any pause or move to a second supplier is a step down. The sell-off that could hammer the stock before the catalyst fully plays out is a third-quarter capacity or qualification slip, or a broad scare over AI capital spending.
positioning_washout: The shareholder base is momentum, retail, and AI-theme investors rather than income or index funds, so there is no mechanical seller from a dividend cut or removal from an index. The forced-selling risk is the opposite: this is a crowded momentum position with a long history of heavy short interest (it has long been a heavily shorted, hard-to-borrow name). A ramp slip or a sector pullback would trigger fast-money and retail capitulation with no valuation floor to catch it (there are no earnings to anchor a price-to-earnings ratio), and the more-than-50% year-over-year growth in share count plus the warrant overhang means every rally meets potential new supply. There is no steady, long-term holder to absorb a flush — which is why the 19% one-day drop on a guidance raise happened. This is a name to buy only after a capitulation, never into strength.
forcing_function: The catalysts that force a re-rating are the quarterly results. Second-quarter fiscal 2026 results (early August 2026) test the roughly 30% sequential guide and second-quarter gross margin (29-30%). Third-quarter fiscal 2026 results (early November 2026) are the key event: they have to show the first leg of the 60-80% sequential ramp and a contribution from Texas capacity, plus evidence that gross margin is moving toward 35%. A clean third-quarter beat re-rates the stock higher; any ramp or qualification slip, or a stall in gross margin, breaks the consensus and re-rates it toward the Fabrinet multiple (5 times EV/sales, enterprise value divided by revenue). Beyond that, confirmation or expansion of the Amazon warrant vesting (purchases toward the $4B trigger) and any named 1.6T design win would be incremental positives — but the catalyst here has already happened and is playing out, rather than being a hard, contractual one still ahead.
moderator_conclusion: The conclusion is to pass for now, at low conviction. Applied Optoelectronics is a real participant in the optical bottleneck of the AI build-out — datacenter revenue up about 154% year-over-year, qualified at Amazon and Microsoft, net cash on the balance sheet, US-based and making its own indium-phosphide lasers — and the demand is genuinely capacity-constrained through mid-2027. But the catalysts that carry the story (the 800G award, the Amazon warrant, the Microsoft agreement, the fiscal 2026 guidance raise) have already happened and are playing out, and they are largely in a roughly $13B price at about 26 times EV/sales (enterprise value divided by revenue). The stock fell 19% on a guidance raise, the textbook sign of a saturated valuation. Our framework penalizes AI-capital-spending theses that have already played out and rewards catalysts that resolve on their own, independent timing — and this thesis hinges on the same single shared driver (the durability of AI capital spending) as the rest of the basket, with no idiosyncratic, dated, lopsided catalyst to set it apart. It is the most volatile, lowest-quality name in its group (the only one losing money, the lowest gross margin, negative return on invested capital, free cash flow of -$418M) and the most stretched per dollar of revenue. Our reconciled base-case value of about $95-115 sits roughly 30-40% below the about $162 market price — the AI upside is already more than fully reflected — against a bear case of about $45-70 (-57% to -72%). The risk and reward are tilted to the downside by roughly two to three times. The catalyst is best described as dated but soft, with no contractual payout: the awards are real but are serial single-customer orders, not binding on the out-year ramp, and the real binary the thesis rides — the second-half 60-80% sequential capacity ramp — is unproven and back-half-loaded. On valuation alone, setting the framework aside, conviction is also low: at about 26 times EV/sales on a money-losing, sub-30%-gross-margin, single-cloud-customer commodity-transceiver vendor, the price is far above intrinsic worth — this is not a value name, it is a momentum growth name at a venture-style multiple. It would only become worth watching for a capitulation if it flushed 40%-plus toward the about $95-115 fair value on a third-quarter ramp scare; at $162 it is a pass. The narrow but real moat (its in-house indium-phosphide laser integration) and the manageable secular threat (commoditization and possible displacement by co-packaged optics) do not justify the multiple.
interrogator_dossier:
# Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) — a deep dive on management and the setup

*Part of the AI build-out optical basket. Drawn from web-gathered first-quarter fiscal 2026 earnings (reported May 7, 2026), prior-quarter context, the fiscal 2021-to-trailing-twelve-months financial path, the balance sheet, and the AI capital-spending backdrop. Price about $162, market value about $13B, as of June 19, 2026.*

## 1. The story so far
Applied Optoelectronics has gone through the most dramatic transformation in the optical group. For a decade the core story was a boom-and-bust cable-TV supplier with a chronically unprofitable, under-scale datacenter transceiver business, persistently short on capacity, repeatedly issuing stock, and dependent on a 100G product cycle that cloud customers kept turning into a commodity. Revenue shrank in 2021 (-9.8%) and 2022 (-2.3%), and the company lost money every year. The pivot took shape across 2025 and into the first quarter of fiscal 2026 as an '800G and 1.6T cloud-scale turning point' story: management recast the company from a cable-modem optics vendor into a US-based, vertically integrated AI-connectivity supplier qualified at the largest cloud customers. The load-bearing claims: demand far exceeds capacity (chief executive Lin: 'actual demand is $1.4-1.5 billion' versus a capacity-constrained guide of more than $1.1B; chief financial officer Murry: 'Forecast demand continues to outpace our production capacity through mid-2027'); vertical integration as an advantage (in-house lasers and indium-phosphide chips — 'AOI has been doing the laser since day one... Indium phosphide capacity is critical right now,' with indium-phosphide wafer capacity up 350% and a move from 4-inch to 6-inch wafers by the end of 2027); a capacity build (from 100,000 units a month exiting the first quarter to more than 650,000 a month by the end of 2026, about 30% US, to more than 930,000 a month by the end of 2027, more than 50% US, reaching a mid-2027 datacenter run-rate of about $471M a month); and co-packaged-optics potential (a new pluggable design at 400,000 units a month by the end of 2027, with three-year supply agreements covering two to three customers). The cable-TV business (44% of the first quarter, up 4%, guided to more than $325M in fiscal 2026) is now the cash cow eclipsed by datacenter (54%, up 154% year-over-year); telecom (2%, down 13%) is being wound down.

## 2. Claims versus the financials
The revenue and growth claims hold up: first-quarter fiscal 2026 revenue of $151.1M (up 51% year-over-year, up 13% quarter-over-quarter), trailing-twelve-month revenue of $507M (up 64%), and a fiscal 2026 guide of more than $1.1B. The margin claims are pointing the right way but are still mid-cycle: gross margin went from 17.8% (fiscal 2021) to 24.8% (fiscal 2024) to 30.0% (fiscal 2025) to 29.2% in the first quarter of fiscal 2026 (it actually dipped versus fiscal 2025), well short of the 35% and 40% targets. The profitability claim is unproven: there has never been an operating profit under standard accounting; the fiscal 2025 operating margin was -12.0%, the trailing-twelve-month figure -6.7%, and the first-quarter non-GAAP loss was $0.07 a share — and the more-than-$140M operating-income guide is non-GAAP and loaded into the second half. The capacity and vertical-integration claim is true but costly: trailing-twelve-month free cash flow is -$418M (-$6.27 a share), and the $440M of cash was funded by a roughly $382M stock raise plus a $125M swap of convertible debt, with dilution of more than 50% year-over-year. The net-cash claim checks out: $439.7M of cash against $244.7M of debt, for about $195M of net cash. The story is richer than the standard-accounting path on profitability, and thinner than the stock implies on near-term cash.

## 3. Tone and confidence
Rising sharply. Management shifted from an apologetic 'demand-recovery' tone to aggressive, quantified, offensive guidance: it named a $124M order book (a $71M 800G tranche since mid-March 2026) and stated explicitly that demand exceeds capacity. Lin's 'we know how to do a good job' on lasers is confident to the point of swagger. The language is offensive throughout; the only defensive notes (indium-phosphide supply, execution) are framed as the company's advantage.

## 4. How credible is the guidance
Mixed. There is a long history of over-promising the datacenter ramp and under-delivering (the 2017-19 100G boom-bust is the template). But recent revenue guidance has been reliable (the first quarter beat its own $150-165M guide, though it missed the Street's $157M). The margin and profitability guidance (35% gross margin, $140M operating income) is the historically slippery forward forecast. Reliable on revenue, weak on margins and timing.

## 5. Where analysts will press
Naming customers (the company won't confirm Amazon or Microsoft, which masks heavy dependence on a single customer); the timing of profitability under standard accounting (the persistent non-GAAP framing); execution risk on capacity (100,000 to 650,000 units a month in about nine months); and dilution (more than 50% year-over-year growth in share count plus 7.95M Amazon warrant shares).

## 6. Warning signs and bright spots
Warning signs: never profitable under standard accounting, with trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of -$418M; more than 50% year-over-year dilution plus the Amazon warrant overhang (7.95M shares at $23.70); about 26 times EV/sales (enterprise value divided by revenue) on money-losing hardware with sub-30% gross margin; datacenter more than 70% of revenue across about two cloud customers; and a 19% drop after earnings on a guidance raise. Bright spots: revenue genuinely inflecting (datacenter up 154%, $124M of named orders); about $195M of net cash; the scarce in-house indium-phosphide and laser asset; and qualification at both Microsoft and Amazon.

## 7. Signals beneath the surface
The shift to 'capacity-constrained' language means the bottleneck has moved from demand to supply, which makes this a manufacturing-execution bet. The '$1.4-1.5B of actual demand' is a planted upside anchor. Introducing co-packaged optics and the new pluggable design seeds the next-generation option and hedges the commoditization that crushed the economics of 100G and 400G. The chief financial officer reframes the cost of indium-phosphide as an advantage — watch whether the 350% wafer expansion lands on time.

## 8. The verdict on capital allocation
The company is mid-transformation and the direction is strengthening but unproven: it is moving from an unprofitable cable-optics microcap to a US AI-connectivity supplier; the revenue inflection is real, the balance sheet is de-risked, but profitability is unproven and free cash flow is -$418M, funded by more than 50% dilution. The thing to weigh: almost all of the AI build-out upside is already in the price — about 26 times EV/sales on a money-losing, two-customer vendor prices the mid-2027 run-rate of $471M a month as if it were certain, while the company has never earned an operating profit under standard accounting and the second-half 60-80% ramp has not yet printed. The moat — cost scale and intangibles from vertical indium-phosphide and laser integration — is genuine but narrow: negative return on invested capital, gross margin of about 29% below Coherent and Credo's roughly 40%-plus, and transceivers that structurally fall in price as cloud customers add second suppliers; its trend is stable (it widens only if indium-phosphide scarcity binds through 2027). On the secular picture, AI build-out connectivity is a durable tailwind and the company is a beneficiary rather than a victim; the genuine long-term risks are displacement by co-packaged optics or silicon photonics within that tailwind, plus commoditization, but they are manageable (the co-packaged-optics hedge plus indium-phosphide scarcity). Cheapness is not the issue — a saturated growth multiple is.

In summary: credibility is moderate (a 3 on a low-to-high scale); the trajectory is turning; the moat is narrow but real; the moat trend is stable; and the secular threat is manageable.
skeptic_kill_fact: There is no single fact that kills the thesis. The bear, pass-for-now case attacks the bull, and every load-bearing bear figure independently checks out against the first-quarter fiscal 2026 8-K filing and SEC filings: a GAAP operating loss (an operating loss under standard accounting rules) of $12.99M (never profitable under standard accounting), a non-GAAP loss of $0.07 a share, a gross margin that dipped to 29.1% under standard accounting (29.2% non-GAAP) from fiscal 2025's 30.0% (so the 35% and 40% targets remain unproven), an Amazon warrant for 7,945,399 shares at $23.6954 (only 1.32M vested upfront, the rest tied to up to $4B of discretionary purchases — that is, soft and contingent, not hard), and datacenter up 154% year-over-year at 54% of $151.1M (more than 70% concentrated in about two cloud customers). At about $162 a share and a roughly $13B market value on about $507M of trailing revenue (about 26 times EV/sales, enterprise value divided by revenue) for a money-losing, sub-30%-gross-margin, single-cloud-customer commodity-transceiver vendor, the AI build-out upside is already fully priced — and the 19% drop on a guidance raise confirms a saturated valuation. The pass verdict survives the adversarial check, and the fair value is not built on a stale anchor: it uses the just-reported first quarter of fiscal 2026 and the raised more-than-$1.1B fiscal 2026 guide.
skeptic_corrections: The thesis was verified, with one correction. Net cash was understated in a conservative direction: the debate cites net cash of about $195M (the dossier figures cash of $439.7M against debt of $244.7M). The actual first-quarter fiscal 2026 8-K shows cash and equivalents of $449.4M and debt of only about $170.7M ($129.5M of convertible senior notes plus $41.2M of current notes payable), for net cash of about $278M — materially higher (less debt) than the debate assumed. This strengthens the balance sheet relative to the thesis but does not change the verdict, since the pass rests on valuation and quality (26 times EV/sales, losses under standard accounting, falling selling prices, customer concentration, and already-priced catalysts) rather than on solvency. Also note that the fiscal 2026 targets of more than $140M in operating income and 35% gross margin are management's non-GAAP guidance commentary, not disclosed in the text of the 8-K — keep them flagged as non-GAAP, second-half-loaded, and unverified in the filing.
skeptic_evidence: AAOI Q1 FY2026 8-K (reported 2026-05-07): total revenue $151.144M, datacenter $81.404M (+154% YoY, 54% of total), CATV $66.841M; GAAP gross margin 29.1% / non-GAAP 29.2%; GAAP operating loss $12.991M; non-GAAP EPS $(0.07); cash $449.377M; debt $129.5M converts + $41.2M current notes (net cash ~$278M); Q2'26 guide $180-198M rev, 29-30% non-GAAP GM (SEC EDGAR / StockTitan 8-K, 2026-05-07)

AAOI Transaction & Warrant Agreement with Amazon, 8-K dated 2026-03-13: warrant for up to 7,945,399 shares @ $23.6954, 1,324,233 vested at issuance, balance vesting on up-to-$4B discretionary Amazon purchases, exercisable through 2035-03-13 (SEC EDGAR, filed Mar 2025)
AAOI Microsoft supply agreement: 5-year initial term, auto-renew, design/manufacture/ship datacenter goods — confirmed in SEC 10-Q FY2025 (SEC EDGAR)
AAOI price ~$161.85 / market cap ~$13.0B on ~80.24M shares outstanding as of 2026-06-19 (stockanalysis.com / tradingeconomics, Jun 2026)

scale.role: Transceiver/laser supplier mid-ramp; design-win-dependent second-tier optical.
scale.rationale: Genuine DataCom traction (+154% YoY = 54% of revenue) but design-win-dependent, never GAAP-profitable (sub-30% GM), and >70% concentrated in ~2 hyperscalers without the LTAs the leaders hold — a TACTICAL franchise at best. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS; the only correction strengthens the balance sheet (net cash ~$278M not ~$195M), an ~8pt haircut off a ~43 franchise. Over-extended (~26x EV/sales on a loss-making commodity vendor); the -19% drop on a guide-RAISE confirms a saturated multiple.
scale.demand_durability: Medium — design-win-dependent, second-tier supplier without the LTAs the leaders have.
